---
title: "Week 5 - Radiant Asymmetry"
subtitle: "Flux delivery, body orientation, and architectural control"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../slides.scss]
    slide-number: true
    center: true
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.07
    transition: fade
    incremental: false
    code-tools: false
---

# Week 5

Radiant asymmetry and flux delivery

**Architecture decides what the body sees**

<span class="tag radiant">A2 due</span>

## Where We Are

::: {.progress-row}
::: {}
A1 spatial air field
:::
::: {.active}
A2 radiant exchange
:::
::: {}
A3 temporal build-up
:::
::: {}
A4 design action
:::
:::

::: {.key}
A2 asks students to translate radiant difference into an architectural claim: not "it feels different," but "this surface, exposure, or orientation changes the body's heat exchange."
:::

## Direction Matters

::: {.split}
::: {}
A body is not a sphere floating in an abstract room.

Radiant asymmetry appears when one side of the body faces a different radiant environment than another side.
:::

::: {.equation-card}
Radiant exchange is shaped by view exposure:

$$
q_{rad,i} \propto F_i(T_i^4 - T_{body}^4)
$$

`F_i` is the fraction of the body's radiant view occupied by surface or source `i`.
:::
:::

## Architectural Flux Delivery

| Architectural element | What it changes | Possible design action |
|---|---|---|
| glass facade | solar gain, longwave exchange, cold/hot surface view | external shade, low-SHGC glazing, seat offset |
| pavement | ground longwave and reflected shortwave load | lighter surface, vegetation, canopy |
| canopy | direct solar exposure and sky view | depth, porosity, orientation |
| exposed slab | thermal storage and surface temperature | insulation, finish, night flushing |
| fan or breeze | convective heat exchange | air path, local control |
| wall material | surface temperature and time lag | finish, insulation, ventilation gap |

## Window Seat Reading

![](../assets/figures/radiant-asymmetry-section.svg){.img-frame}

::: {.caption}
Use the section as a reasoning template: position, body orientation, surface field, and design action.
:::

## Indoor Versus Outdoor Radiant Stakes

::: {.cards-3}
::: {.example}
**Indoor**

Radiant asymmetry may be subtle but persistent: cold glass, warm perimeter, radiant panels, exposed slab.
:::

::: {.example}
**Outdoor**

Solar radiation and hot ground can dominate within seconds or minutes.
:::

::: {.example}
**Threshold**

Arcade, lobby, balcony, and entry conditions often mix air-conditioned expectations with outdoor radiant exposure.
:::
:::

## A2 Card Structure

::: {.artifact}
Your Radiant Exchange Card must include:

1. two occupied positions;
2. air temperature comparison;
3. surface/radiant/MRT proxy evidence;
4. body orientation diagram;
5. one heat-flux or exchange sketch;
6. one architectural implication;
7. one uncertainty statement.
:::

## What A2 Should Not Do

::: {.warning}
A2 should not become a generic comfort essay or a polished rendering with no thermal mechanism.

If the drawing cannot answer "what exchange changed?", it is not yet an A2 argument.
:::

## Mini-Case: Shaded Bench

::: {.split}
::: {}
Two benches show similar air temperature:

- Bench A is under tree shade on lighter paving.
- Bench B is under partial glass reflection near dark paving.

A2 asks: what does the body receive from above, below, and around?
:::

::: {.equation-card}
The simplified comparison:

$$
\Delta q_{rad} \approx h_rA(T_{mrt,B}-T_{mrt,A})
$$

If the MRT proxy differs substantially, the design implication may be shade quality, surface material, or seating relocation.
:::
:::

## Session 2: Diagnostic Round

::: {.round-steps}
::: {.round-step}
**10 min - case scan.** Choose a design image where body geometry matters: one side of the body faces a different radiant environment.
:::
::: {.round-step}
**5 min - Slack post.** Post the image and identify the body posture or orientation. Keep the thermal diagnosis hidden.
:::
::: {.round-step}
**20-25 min - round-table guesses.** Classmates guess the exposed body side, radiant source or sink, design mitigation, and competing constraint.
:::
::: {.round-step}
**10-15 min - host reveal.** The host explains the actual radiant asymmetry concern and whether the current design response is sufficient.
:::
:::

## Week 5 Hint Level

::: {.hint-card thinner}
Moderate hints.

Do not treat the body as a point. Guess:

- face, back, legs, head, feet, hands;
- seated, standing, waiting, moving;
- one-sided glass, pavement, wall, sky, canopy, or radiant panel;
- what orientation or furniture change would alter exposure.
:::

## Diagnostic Translation

::: {.activity}
Turn the host reveal into a radiant exchange card:

- body segment or side exposed;
- source or sink;
- design lever;
- competing constraint;
- uncertainty.
:::

## Exit Artifact

::: {.artifact}
Write:

> This radiant condition may fail when `_____` changes over time.
:::

## Carry Forward

Next week we stop treating thermal conditions as static. Heat can build, lag, store, and remain risky after the obvious driver has passed.
